coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots and its use as a novel optical probe for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Xiaoyan, Zhou
Zhangyi, Li
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: The functionalization of graphene quantum dots has become a powerful method to modulate its chemical, electronic and optical properties for various applications. In the study, we reported a facile synthesis of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Val-GQDs) and its use as a novel fluorescent probe for optical detection of Hg2+. Herein, Val-GQDs was synthesized by the thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and valine. The resulting Val-GQDs has an average size of 3nm and the edge of graphene sheets contains the rich of hydrophilic groups, leading to a high water-solubility. Compared to the GQDs prepared by thermal pyrolysis of citric acid, Val-GQDs exhibits a stronger fluorescence (>10-fold) and better photostability (>4-fold). Interestingly, the existence of valine moieties in the Val-GQDs results in a more sensitive fluorescent response to Hg2+. The fluorescent signal will linearly decrease with the increase of Hg2+ concentration in the range from 0.8nM to 1μM with the correlation coefficient of 0.992. The detection limit is 0.4nM (S/N=3), which the sensitivity is >14-fold that of GQDs. The analytical method provides the prominent advantage of sensitivity, selectivity and stability. It has been successfully applied in the optical detection of Hg2+ in real water samples. The study also provides a promising approach for the design and synthesis of functionalized GQDs to meet the needs of further applications in sensing and catalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent probe
Hg2+
Detection
Water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Procedure for fluorescence detection of mercury ions
Synthesis of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Effect of pH and reaction time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of valine amount on fluorescence intensity.
Effect of reaction time on fluorescence intensity (A) and absorption spectra (B) of Val-GQDs.
TEM image (A) and IR spectrum (B) of Val-GQDs. Inset: size distribution of Val-GQDs. AFM image (C) and height distributions (D) of Val-GQDs as well as their corresponding height profile (E).
The total (A), C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D) XPS spectra of Val-GQDs.
The fluorescence excitation spectra (a) and emission spectra (b) of GQDs (A) and Val-GQDs (B).
The fluorescence spectra of Val-GQDs at excitation wavelengths from (a to g) 300 to 360 (A) and (g to l) 360 to 410nm (B).
The fluorescence retention rate (F/F0) of Val-GQDs (a) and GQDs (b) with different irradiation time.
The fluorescent spectra of Val-GQDs (A) and GQDs (B) before (upper) and after (down) added 2μM Hg2+. Insert: fluorescent images of Val-GQDs before (left) and after (right) the addition of Hg2+.
The change of fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of different Val-GQDs in the presence of 2μM Hg2+.
The effect of pH (A) and reaction time (B) on fluorescence intensity changes of GQDs (a) and Val-GQDs in the presence of Hg2+.

A. Fluorescence spectra of Val-GQDs in the presence of 0, 0.0008, 0.0016, 0.002, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.16, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 5, 10, 16, 20μM Hg2+ (from top to bottom). B: plots of the values of F/F0 vs. the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.0008–20μM. Insert: calibration plots of Hg2+ concentration vs. F/F0.

Fluorescence changes of Val-GQDs in the presence of different metal ions (A) and fluorescence response of val-GQDs towards 2μM Hg2+ (black) and interference of 1μM other metal ions with 1μM Hg2+ (red) (B).
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Hg2+ in water samples (N=5).

row.entry: Found Hg2+ by proposed method CVAAS (nM)

head.ce:title: Fabrication of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots and its use as a novel optical probe for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The functionalization of graphene quantum dots has become a powerful method to modulate its chemical, electronic and optical properties for various applications. In the study, we reported a facile synthesis of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Val-GQDs) and its use as a novel fluorescent probe for optical detection of Hg2+. Herein, Val-GQDs was synthesized by the thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and valine. The resulting Val-GQDs has an average size of 3nm and the edge of graphene sheets contains the rich of hydrophilic groups, leading to a high water-solubility. Compared to the GQDs prepared by thermal pyrolysis of citric acid, Val-GQDs exhibits a stronger fluorescence (>10-fold) and better photostability (>4-fold). Interestingly, the existence of valine moieties in the Val-GQDs results in a more sensitive fluorescent response to Hg2+. The fluorescent signal will linearly decrease with the increase of Hg2+ concentration in the range from 0.8nM to 1μM with the correlation coefficient of 0.992. The detection limit is 0.4nM (S/N=3), which the sensitivity is >14-fold that of GQDs. The analytical method provides the prominent advantage of sensitivity, selectivity and stability. It has been successfully applied in the optical detection of Hg2+ in real water samples. The study also provides a promising approach for the design and synthesis of functionalized GQDs to meet the needs of further applications in sensing and catalysis.

We reported a facile synthesis of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots by pyrolysis of citric acid and valine. The as-prepared valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots offer an excellent optical property and was successfully used to the detection of Hg2+ by fluorescence method with good rapidity, sensitivity and selectivity.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Because of high toxicity and severe threat to ecological system and human health, heavy metal contamination has become increasingly serious along with the industrial development, and attracted much attention throughout the world [1]. The investigation has demonstrated that mercury, one of the most toxic heavy metals, has recognized accumulative and persistent characters, which can accumulate in vital organs and tissues of animal and human body through the skin, gastrointestinal or respiratory [2–4]. Long-term exposure to mercury, even at a very low concentration, will have an adverse effects on human health and environment. The accumulated mercury in the human body over time ultimately causes digestive, kidney and especially neurological diseases [5,6]. In addition, the most common forms of water soluble mercury can exist stable in water [7]. All these potential threats make mercury contamination become a global environmental problem [8]. To protect environment and human health, the United States Environment Protection Agency proposed the upper limit of Hg2+ in drinking water at 10nM [9]. There is a great need to develop reliable analytical method for rapid detection of Hg2+ with high sensitivity.

Many analytical technologies have been well developed for the detection of trace Hg2+, including atomic absorption/emission spectrometry (AAS/AES) [10,11], inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [12], surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [13], flow injection technique (FIA) [14] and electrochemical method [15]. These methods offer the advantages of high accuracy, but they usually require high costs, large amounts of samples, sophisticated instruments and complicated sample preparation processes, which largely limit their practical applications [16]. In the recent years, the fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection was given a special attention on the account of its low-cost, fast response, high selectivity and sensitivity [17]. The performance of fluorescent material play key roles in the fabrication of fluorescent sensors. It not only determines the stability of the sensors to a certain extent, but also seriously affects the sensitivity and selectivity of the analytical methods. Hence, choosing an appropriate fluorescent reagent is the most important work for the specific fluorescent assay. Organic fluorophores, semiconductor quantum dots [18], and metal nanoclusters [19–21] are typical fluorescent materials for Hg2+ sensors. However, the present fluorescent sensors based on the above fluorescent materials are difficult to be widely applied to routine analysis due to their intrinsic limitations. Often, organic fluorophore suffers from relatively low photo-stability and weak fluorescence, seriously affecting their sensitivity and accuracy for the optical detection. Semiconductor quantum dots contain heavy metals as essential elements, which bring up dangers to the organisms and environment. Although metal nanoclusters such as gold, silver and copper nanoclusters can replace noxious semiconductor quantum dots due to their comparable fluorescence intensity and good biocompatibility, they still lack of sufficient yield and optical properties. There is a great need to develop high-performance fluorescent materials for fluorescent sensing. More recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as new emerging fluorescent materials has drawn a great deal of research interest owing to its fantastic characteristic compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots, including low cytotoxicity, robust chemical inertness, low photo-bleaching, and excellent biocompatibility [22,23]. The investigations have demonstrated that GQDs has a great prospect in applications of bio-imaging [23], catalysis and photocatalysis [24,25], and energy conversion and storage [26], particularly as attractive alternatives of sensing material for various fluorescent assay [27]. To date, many fluorescent sensors based on GQDs for Hg2+ detection have been developed. However, present GQDs-based fluorescent sensors surfer from no ideal photo-stability and sensitivity for detection of trace Hg2+ [28]. These deficiencies greatly limit their applications in sensing. Many ways have been successfully attempted to enhance the fluorescent performance of GQDs. Among these, the chemical functionalization of GQDs is the most effective mean for modifying their chemical, electronic and optical properties [28]. It can also introduce some specific chemical moieties, which would improve the fluorescent efficiency and selectivity of GQDs for the detection of metal ions [29,30]. The preparation of functionalized graphene quantum dots usually requires two steps, namely, the formation of graphene quantum dots and the subsequent functionalization. As chemically active groups in the GQDs lack of enough stability and are easy to fall off from the graphene sheets in the functionalization process. This makes the final product contains less functional groups or structures, thus leading to a slow reaction rate and low sensitivity for their applications in the detection of Hg2+ or other metal ions.

In the study, we reported a facile synthesis of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (termed as Val-GQDs). The Val-GQDs was prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid and valine, in which the formation of graphene quantum dots and its functionalization is completed in one step with high yield. The resulted Val-GQDs offers a stronger fluorescence, better photo-stability, and more sensitive and selective fluorescent response to Hg2+ compared to the GQDs obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid. The optical sensor based on the Val-GQDs has been successfully applied in ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in real water samples.

Citric acid monohydrate, l-valine, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and mercuric sulfate were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were analytical grade without further purification. Britton-Robinson buffer solution (BR buffer, H3PO4–HAc–H3BO3, 0.04M) was prepared in the laboratory and its pH value was adjusted using 0.2M NaOH solution. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) purified from Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

UV–visible spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 spectrometer with a DH-2000 deuterium and tungsten halogen light source in absorbance mode (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China). Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, America). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was obtained by a JEOL 2010 FEG microscope at 200KeV. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was obtained on a Multimode 8 force microscope (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out on a PHI 5700 ESCA spectrometer using Al HR radiation (hν =1486.6eV). Infrared spectrum (IR) was recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis, 0.5g of citric acid was dissolved in 1ml of ultrapure water, then 0.25g of valine was added into the above solution under sonication. The mixture was then heated at 200°C for 2.5h with a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1. After cooling down to room temperature, the collected solid product was dissolved in 1M NaOH solution, and its acidity was slowly adjusted to pH7. Finally, the solution was dialyzed in ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane for 48h to remove unreacted reagents. The solid product was obtained by freeze drying and stored at 4°C for further use. The procedure was also used for preparation of other Val-GQDs samples with different valine amounts. For comparison, a control sample (termed as GQDs) was also prepared using the same procedure unless no addition of valine.

The photo-stability experiment of Val-GQDs and GQDs samples was carried in a 20ml vial filled with different GQDs under a xenon lamp irradiation (300W). At given time intervals, 1ml of the solution was collected and then its fluorescence spectrum was measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The quantum yield (Φ) of Val-GQDs was calculated by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities and absorbance values (<0.05) of the GQDs samples with the reference quinine sulfate [31]. The absorbance for quinine sulfate and Val-GQDs were measured in a TU-1901 spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra of the samples were recorded under the same testing conditions. Then the integrated fluorescence intensity from fluorescence spectrum was calculated. At last the quantum yield of Val-GQDs (in water) was calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
Φ =
Φ r
m η
2
m r
η r 2
where Φ is the quantum yield of Val-GQDs, Φ
r is the quantum yield of quinine sulfate, m is the slope obtained from the linearizing plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance of Val-GQDs, m
r is the slope of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance of quinine sulfate, η and η
r are the refractive indexes of the sample and reference, respectively. The subscript r refers to the reference Quinine sulfate (literature Φ =0.54). Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (η =1.33) and Val-GQDs were dissolved in ultrapure water (η =1.33).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for fluorescence detection of mercury ions
ce:section.ce:para: A 200μl of Val-GQDs solution (160μgml−1) was added in a 2ml centrifuge tube containing 600μl BR buffer (pH6.5) and then mixed well. To this solution, 200μl of Hg2+ standard solution with a known concentration or a real water sample was added. After 5min of incubation at room temperature, fluorescence spectrum of the solution was recorded by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 360nm. The fluorescent test for each Hg2+ concentration was repeated thrice, and the average fluorescent signal was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The functionalization of GQDs has attracted increasing attention, because the functionalization can be an effective method to enhance their functionality and modify their electronic, chemical and optical properties of GQDs, which will further expand their applications. Typically, the synthesis of functionalized GQDs involves the preparation of GQDs and the follow-up functionalization of GQDs [30,32,33]. However, the two-step method is often time-consuming and lead to the quenching of fluorescence. Moreover, GQDs is a large conjugated system, which causes some oxygen-containing groups on the edge are not stable enough and thus tending to fall off during the functionalization process, leading to a relatively low functionalization degree. Such a low degree of functionality often lacks of rich functional groups, which causes a slower reaction rate or a low sensitivity. In this work, a one-step thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and valine was used for the fabrication of Val-GQDs. During the formation of Val-GQDs, the formation of GQDs and its functionalization simultaneously occurred. In the process, the carbon source, critic acid, plays an essential role in ensuring the formation of GQDs through the dehydration and carbonization, while valine was mainly designed as a functional agent to improve the optical property and the functionality of GQDs. The functional groups could be in-situ introduced into the edges of GQDs through the covalent interaction between the two small molecules. The strong interaction renders Val-GQDs a more stable structure compared with functionalization of GQDs by two-step methods. This provides a promising approach for the design and synthesis of novel functionalized GQDs with an enhanced performance.

A proper amount of functional reagent is essential to the surface functional groups and fluorescence properties of the functionalized GQDs. Less amount of valine leads to GQDs with perfect structure, but with fewer functional groups due to incomplete functionalization. However, excess valine may consume too much citric acids, leading to a smaller GQDs sheets or even cannot form GQDs. It will also destroy the integrity of GQDs and result in quenching of fluorescence to some extent. To find a suitable amount of valine, other Val-GQDs were prepared by tuning the amount of valine while fixing citric acid of 0.5g. The relationship of the fluorescence intensity with the amount of valine is presented in Fig. 1 . All GQDs functionalized with valine enhanced the GQDs fluorescence to a certain extent, suggesting indicating that the addition of valine can remarkably enhance the fluorescence intensity. When the amount of valine in the precursor is increased gradually from 0 to 2.5g, the fluorescence peak around 450nm increases. Then the fluorescence intensity reaches a maximum value when valine is up to 0.25g. However, continuous increase of valine amount causes a decrease in intensity. This may be because excess valine may disturb the polymerization of citric acid and decrease the conjugation of the product, which results in fluorescent quenching. To obtain excellent fluorescence emission, 0.25g of valine was used for the synthesis of Val-GQDs in the following experiments.

Further, the formation process of Val-GQDs was systematically investigated by measuring the fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra at various reaction time. Fig. 2A presents the fluorescence intensity of Val-GQDs with different reaction times. In the initial stage, the intensity rapidly increases with the increase of reaction time. When the reaction time increases to 2.5h, the fluorescence intensity reaches its maximum value. However, further prolonging of reaction time causes a rapid decrease in fluorescence intensity due to a higher carbonization degree. Fig. 2B gives the absorption spectra of Val-GQDs taken from 0 to 5h (from bottom to up). It can be observed that an absorption peak at 345nm appeared at the reaction time of 10min, indicating the formation of Val-GQDs. Prolonging the reaction time, the absorbance of Val-GQDs increases progressively, indicating the continuous formation of Val-GQDs. Moreover, Fig. 2B also exhibits that the intensity of the absorption peak of Val-GQDs is slightly blue-shift. This may be because large-sized polymer nanoparticles generated in the initial stage and the polymer nanoparticles shrunk through dehydration, which results in a decreased size, leading to a blue-shift absorbance. To obtain high fluorescence intensity, the reaction time of 2.5h was employed for the synthesis of Val-GQDs.

The as-prepared Val-GQDs was characterized by TEM, AFM, IR and XPS technologies. The TEM analysis (Fig. 3A) shows that Val-GQDs exhibits excellent uniformity with low degree of agglomeration. The insert of Fig. 3A presents the particle size distribution of Val-GQDs. The size of most Val-GQDs is in the range of 1–4nm with the average size of 3nm. The corresponding AFM image in Fig. 3C demonstrates that their average topographic heights were 1.2nm, indicating most of the Val-GQDs primarily consist of 1–2 graphene layers. FTIR was used to investigate the functional groups of Val-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3B, the broad absorption bands at 3430cm−1 is a characteristic peak of the stretching vibration of OH. The band around 1700cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO. The peak centered at 2970 and 1080cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration CH and CO groups. In addition, the peaks at 3250, 2360 and 1210cm−1 indicate the existence of the N-related bonds. The above analysis demonstrates that the resulting Val-GQDs was surrounded mainly by amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups.

In order to further investigate the surface chemical properties of Val-GQDs, XPS technology was used to measure their element type and chemical state. Fig. 4A indicates that the total XPS spectrum of Val-GQDs gives three peaks at 284.4, 401 and 531.9eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. On the C1s XPS spectrum there are three peaks. The peaks located at 284.5, 286.6 and 288.1eV can be assigned to CC, CO/CN and CO species, respectively. There are two peaks at 399.5 and 401.2eV, respectively. The peak at 399.5eV can be assigned to amide, amine or pyrrolic N. The peak at 401.2eV corresponds to graphitic N. This reveals that the nitrogen replaced carbon in GQDs graphene sheet and was incorporated into the carbon network [34]. These results demonstrates that Val-GQDs has been successfully achieved by functionalization.

Val-GQDs gives higher fluorescence intensity compared with GQDs prepared by using citric acid solely. The intensity is 10-fold higher than that of GQDs, which indicates the introduction of valine can remarkably enhance the fluorescence intensity. In addition, the relative fluorescence quantum yield of Val-GQDs was measured to be 28.07% using quinine sulfate as a standard, which is higher than that of pristine GQDs (7.6%). To understand enhanced fluorescent performance with functionalization, we study on the fluorescence excitation and emission performance of Val-GQDs and GQDs. Often, the fluorescence excitation and emission features reflect the characteristics of the material structure and the energy level of the electron, respectively. From Fig. 5 , pristine GQDs have optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 422nm and 475nm, respectively. When valine was introduced, the excitation and emission wavelengths blue-shift to 360nm and 450nm. The blue shift and the above enhanced intensity in fluorescence of Val-GQDs should originate from the different size and electronic structure in graphene sheets of Val-GQDs due to the introduction of valine [35]. The steric hindrance of valine decrease the degree of polymerization of citric acid, leading to a smaller Val-GQDs. The blue-shifted fluorescence emission confirms that the amino species in Val-GQDs are the pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N atoms. This because the relatively strong electron withdrawing ability of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N atoms would bring about blue-shift of fluorescence emission. However, primary amino groups attached at the edge of sp2 cluster will result in the red-shift fluorescence emission due to their abilities of increasing the electron density of GQDs and decreasing the band gap [36]. Besides, the narrow fluorescence spectrum of Val-GQDs indicates a uniformity of size distribution compared with that of pristine GQDs. Therefore, the enhancement of the fluorescence properties of Val-GQDs should be attributed to both dimension variation and surface chemical change.

To further explore the optical properties of as-synthesized Val-GQDs, detailed fluorescent spectra obtained by changing excitation wavelengths from 300 to 410nm was shown in Fig. 6 . It can be seen that the changes in excitation wavelength from 300 to 380nm does not cause a notable shift of maximum emission peak position. Only when the excitation wavelength beyond 390nm, an obvious red-shift was observed. The strongest emission peak centered at 450nm was observed when Val-GQDs was excited at 360nm. Such excitation-dependent fluorescence suggests both the size and surface state of Val-GQDs are relatively uniform [37].

The application of Val-GQDs as a novel optical probe requires a strong fluorescence intensity and high stability. The photo-stability of Val-GQDs and GQDs was investigated in the study. As shown in Fig. 7 , with the increase of irradiation time, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreases. The fluorescence intensity decreases by about 70% under continuous irradiation for 100min, indicating GQDs has a relatively poor photo-stability due to an unstable surface states. However, the fluorescence intensity of Val-GQDs can keep almost unchanged in the same period of irradiation time, indicating an excellent photo-stability. The photo-stability of Val-GQDs is 4-fold higher than that of GQDs. Such an excellent photo-stability should be attributed the uniform and stable surface states resulting from the introduction of valine.

Val-GQDs can act as a new kind of fluorescent probe for detection of metal ions. Fig. 8A presents the fluorescence spectra of Val-GQDs in the presence and absence of 2μM Hg2+. The fluorescence intensity of Val-GQDs displays a remarkably quenching after the addition of 2μM Hg2+. The change of fluorescence intensity (ΔF) is up to 730 (about 88.2%). It can be seen from insert of Fig. 8A the blue fluorescence of Val-GQDs quenched upon the addition of Hg2+ under 365nm irradiation. This indicates Val-GQDs gives a highly sensitive fluorescent response towards Hg2+. GQDs was employed as a control to test the fluorescence response. Fig. 8B shows that the addition of 2μM Hg2+ only brings a smaller variation in fluorescent signal (6.2%). The result verifies that Val-GQDs has more sensitive response towards Hg2+ compared with GQDs, for which the sensitivity of Val-GQDs is 14-fold higher than that of GQDs. This is because the low fluorescence intensity of GQDs leads to a relatively low sensitivity response compared with Val-GQDs. More importantly, the enhanced quenching effect could be also attributed to the stronger interaction between Val-GQDs and Hg2+ to form stable complexes, which can accelerate the non-radiative recombination of the exciton through an effective electron transfer process, leading to the quenching of the fluorescence.

The above enhancement of affinity of Val-GQDs for Hg2+ should be attributed to the introduction of nitrogen atoms of valine into GQDs. To further investigate the quenching effect, the fluorescence change of Val-GQDs with different valine amount in the presence of Hg2+ was tested. Fig. 9 shows the quenching efficiency increased with the increase of valine amount and reached a maximum quenching efficiency when the amount of valine is 0.25g, which is corresponding to the strongest fluorescence intensity. A further increase of valine amount resulted in a decreased quenching efficiency. The results indicate that the introduction of valine in the synthesis obviously improves the fluorescence response towards Hg2+. On the one hand, the interaction between Hg2+ and the amino groups attached on the surface of Val-GQDs to form HgN coordinate bond, which will result in the quenching of Val-GQDs. On the other hand, the presence of nitrogen should effectively tune the electronic density of Val-GQDs, which greatly promote the coordination interaction between oxygen functional groups on the edge of Val-GQDs and Hg2+. The fast electron transfer process and promoted interaction, therefore, cause significant fluorescence quenching of Val-GQDs. In addition, the enhanced fluorescence intensity of Val-GQDs also leads to a relativity higher sensitivity Hg2+ response compared with GQDs. The above factors make Val-GQDs a more sensitive fluorescent response towards Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and reaction time
ce:section.ce:para: The acidity of the reaction medium not only affects the fluorescence intensity of GQDs but also the formation of metal complexes [38,39]. Herein, we investigated the effect of pH on the fluorescence quenching of Val-GQDs in the presence of 2μM Hg2+ in the BR buffer (pH2–12). Fig. 10A shows that the quenching efficiency was significantly dependent on solution pH value. The quenching efficiency increased rapidly with pH increase from 2 to 6.5 and then reached its highest value at pH6.5. However, an obvious decline in quenching efficiency was observed when the pH beyond 6.5. Clearly, the quenching efficiency is lower in the acidic or alkaline conditions. These can be explained as follows. At a lower pH, the amino and carboxyl groups in the Val-GQDs were well protonated. Hg2+ can be combined with Val-GQDs to form a stable complex, leading to a relatively lower quenching. When the pH increased, the protonation of functional groups is suppressed, which is more favorable for the complex formation, leading to higher quenching. However, Hg2+ would give priority to reacts with the OH− to form Hg(OH)2 under alkaline conditions, which readily converts to the HgO [40]. Consequently, the fluorescence quenching for Hg2+ would be significantly weakened under alkaline condition. Considering the best quenching efficiency, the following experimental condition was set at pH6.5.

To test the response time of the as-prepared Val-GQDs to Hg2+, Val-GQDs solution was well mixed with Hg2+ and then its fluorescent spectrum was immediately measured using fluorophotometer. As shown in Fig. 10B, the fluorescence intensity of Val-GQDs decreases rapidly and reaches its maximum quenching value within 30s of contact time, indicating the reaction could be completed within 30s. A further increase of reaction time does not lead to any further detectable fluorescent quenching. The fluorescence intensity can remain almost unchanged over the course of at least of 60min, indicating an excellent stability for Hg2+ detection. The result also indicates that Val-GQDs display a very fast response to Hg2+, which the rate is 10-fold higher than that of pristine GQDs. The fact reveals that the introduction of valine will remarkably accelerate the reaction of GQDs with Hg2+. A contact time of 5min was selected for the detection.

The sensor was repeatedly measured ten times in a 0.2μM of Hg2+ standard solution under the same conditions. A relative standard deviation of 2.4% for the measurements was obtained, indicating good precision. The Val-GQDs product was stored at 4°C and its sensitivity for the detection of Hg2+ was checked every week. The fluorescence intensity remains the initial response of 99.6% at least after the period of four weeks, indicating an excellent long-term stability.

The selectivity is one of the most important factors to evaluate the analytical performance of the proposed method. Herein, the effect of various metallic and nonmetallic ions on the fluorescence intensity of Val-GQDs was investigated and the result was shown in Fig. 12 . In addition to the amino and carboxyl groups, there are only two methyl groups and no any other functional groups in the valine molecule. During the thermal pyrolysis, the most of amino and carboxyl groups in the valine and carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in citric acid were be decomposed and consumed for the fabrication of Val-GQDs. From the results of IR and XPS analysis, we know that only small amount of amino and carboxyl groups exist on the edge of the final Val-GQDs. For Val-GQD its amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl on the edge and graphene sheets constitute an unique spatial configuration. The structural characteristic determines that Val-GQDs can be combined with the metal ions via the coordination bond with amino and electrovalent bond with carboxyl groups. However, the interaction is very weak due to its small amount of functional groups. Only special metal ions, which have a perfect matching in the spatial configuration of Val-GQDs and strongly interact with the amino and carboxyl groups, can form stable compound and lead to an obvious fluorescence quenching.

Among metallic ions studied, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Ba2+ offer a much weaker affinity with amino and carboxy groups. Their addition doesn't produce stable complex and results in an obvious change in the fluorescence signal. Thus, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Ba2+ can not interfere with the determination of Hg2+. Ni2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ag+ and Zn2+ can interact with amino and hydroxyl groups to form corresponding four-ligands complex in the neutral reaction medium. However, small number of functional groups on the Val-GQDs greatly reduces the ability to form the complex. The result shows that no obvious change in the fluorescence signal can be observed in the test system, indicating a high selectivity. Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ are of the strong ability to form insoluble salt with carboxyl or sulfydryl groups via the covalent bonds. As a rule, carboxyl group indicates a relative weak affinity with the above ions compared to mercapto groups. However, the nitrogen in the graphene sheets of Val-GQDs will obviously improve the electron density of carboxyl groups. This reduces the polarity of the OH bond in the carboxyl group and decreases the ability to form covalent bonds between the carboxyl on the Val-GQDs and the above metallic ions. The result demonstrates that only Hg2+ can react with the carboxyl and amino groups in the Val-GQDs to form stable compound under the selected reaction conditions due to its best affinity among Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+. Thus, Ag+ and Cu2+ doesn't interfere with the fluorescent detection of Hg2+. In addition, Fig. 12B shows that no change in the fluorescence response of Val-GQDs towards Hg2+ was observed in the presence of other metallic ions. The stability of Hg-Val-GQDs is much better than other metallic ions, and Hg2+ is easy to replace other metallic ions from corresponding complexes formed. Thus, the existence of other metallic ions doesn't interfere with the detection of Hg2+.

The tolerance limit of co-existing ions, which caused a change of 5% in the peak fluorescence intensity, was measured and the results were listed in Table s1. The 2000-fold K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, and Cl− do not interfere with the detection. Ni2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+ are at trace level in environmental water, and 100-fold Ni2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+ do not interfere with the detection. In general, the excellent selectivity to Hg2+ could be attributed to the unique structure of Val-GQDs. The structure make only Hg2+ can combine with Val-GQDs to form stable complex via the coordination bond and covalent bond in all co-existing ions studied, indicating a high sensitivity to Hg2+ and excellent selectivity.

To explore the practical applications of the newly developed method, possible applications was investigated by analyzing real water samples. Three water samples including well water, lake water and rain water were spiked with known concentrations of Hg2+. Followed by analyzed by the developed method. The concentration of Hg2+ in the sample was determined from the calibration curve and the value was used to calculate the concentration in the original sample. The results of the detection of Hg2+ in water samples are listed in Table 2 . The recovery of Hg2+ for spiked samples analysis is in the range of 99.3–100.5%. There is a very good agreement between the results obtained by the proposed method and cold vapor atomic adsorption spectrometry method (CVAAS) [44]. This indicated the proposed method has a good accuracy and precision.

In this study, Val-GQDs has been successfully synthesized and applied for the detection of Hg2+. The experiment demonstrates that the content of valine has a significant influence on the fluorescence intensity of Val-GQDs and the sensitivity of fluorescent response towards Hg2+. The introduction of nitrogen will result in largely enhanced the reaction rate of Val-GQDs with Hg2+. The sensor based on Val-GQDs exhibits excellent long-term stability, sensitivity, repeatability and stability. More importantly, the study opens a new avenue for the fabrication of various functionalized GQDs, which holds great potential applications in sensing, bio-imaging and catalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2016.08.037.
